behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on real filesystem watcher semantics for nested paths and proving an external side effect (the file is actually processed end-to-end). Unit tests of path logic or recursion flags can pass while real OS watcher behavior still fails.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon against a real temporary watched directory, create a nested subdirectory, write a file there, and verify via output/readback that exactly one transformed result is produced for that nested file.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily module handoff/state behavior (watch/request intake + dedup cache/window + pipeline dispatch). It does not require real OS/network/subprocess if requests can be injected in-process with controlled time.
behaviors.test_description: Wire real intake/dedup/pipeline modules together in-process, submit two requests for the same path within the configured window, and assert only one pipeline execution is recorded; then assert a later request outside the window is accepted.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is explicitly OS event duplication behavior. In-process fakes can miss real event burst/coalescing patterns; you must validate exactly-once processing against real watcher events and external output effect.
behaviors.test_description: With the daemon running on a real watched directory, perform a single file write known to trigger multiple FS events on the target OS, then verify only one processing result is produced (and no duplicate output/dispatch).

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Directory deletion/recreation and watcher reattachment are OS-level behaviors. The requirement is an externally visible recovery effect during continuous runtime, which must be proven through real filesystem round-trip.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real watched directory, delete that directory, recreate it at the same path, add a new file, and verify the daemon processes the new file without restarting the process.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering here depends on real filesystem metadata timing and event arrival interactions under concurrency. Mocked clocks/events can hide timestamp resolution and event sequencing issues seen in real OS/filesystem behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Create multiple files nearly simultaneously in the real watched directory with controlled creation-time differences, then verify processing order in emitted outputs/logged dispatches matches creation timestamp order.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: This is a real filesystem timing/partial-write condition. The bug appears at OS I/O boundary (event timing vs write completion), and correctness requires observing the external processing side effect only after write completion.
behaviors.test_description: Write a large file in chunks to the real watched directory while the daemon runs; assert no processing occurs mid-write, then assert processing starts only after the writer closes/completes and output corresponds to complete file contents.
